label: Extended Mind
definition: The thesis, argued by Andy Clark and David Chalmers in 1998, that the mind is not confined to the brain but can literally extend into the tools and environment a person uses, when those external resources play the same functional role as internal cognition. Its engine is the parity principle: if a process inside the head would count as cognitive, the same process outside the head should too, which makes Otto's notebook part of his mind and the human-plus-tool the unit of intelligence.

body.text: The extended mind thesis holds that cognitive processes are not always contained inside the skull. It was proposed by Andy Clark and David Chalmers in their 1998 paper "The Extended Mind". Their claim is bold and meant literally: when a notebook, a smartphone, or a sketchpad plays the right functional role in someone's thinking, that external object is not merely a tool the mind uses but a genuine part of the cognitive process itself. The boundary of the mind, on this view, is not the boundary of the body. It is wherever the machinery doing the cognitive work happens to lie, a position the authors call active externalism, because the external resource is actively coupled to the agent in real time.

The argument rests on what Clark and Chalmers call the parity principle, the keeper. If a process were going on inside the head, and we would not hesitate to call it cognitive, then the same process going on outside the head should count as cognitive too. To dramatize it they offer Otto and Inga. Inga wants to go to the museum, recalls from biological memory that it is on a certain street, and goes. Otto has a memory impairment and writes everything in a notebook he always carries; he wants to go to the museum, looks up the address in the notebook, and goes. The notebook plays for Otto exactly the role that biological memory plays for Inga: it stores information, is reliably available, is automatically endorsed, and is consulted as needed. If Inga's belief about the address was a standing belief stored in memory, then by parity Otto's belief is stored in the notebook. The notebook is part of Otto's mind. This reasoning is explicitly functionalist: what makes a state mental is the role it plays, not the stuff it is made of, which ties the thesis to functionalism and to the computational-theory-of-mind.

The thesis sits within the broader "4E" movement in cognitive science, which holds that cognition is embodied, embedded, enacted, and extended, and it is a close cousin of embodied-cognition. Where embodiment stresses that thinking is shaped by having a body, extension stresses that thinking can recruit the world beyond the body. Both push against the classical picture of the mind as an inner symbol-processing engine sealed off from its surroundings.

The main objection, pressed hardest by Fred Adams and Kenneth Aizawa and by Robert Rupert, is the "mark of the cognitive" challenge. Critics argue that genuine cognition involves intrinsic content, the kind of original intentionality a notebook's ink does not have, and that the parity principle proves too much: if any reliable external information store counts as part of the mind, the mind balloons to absurdity, swallowing libraries and the internet. The standard reply distinguishes a cognitive process that is coupled to a resource from one that merely uses it, and adds conditions, constancy, ready accessibility, automatic endorsement, to keep the boundary principled rather than limitless. Critics counter that "coupling" is not the same as "constituting", and that the resource only matters because of what the brain does with it.

For AI the extended mind reframes what a thinking system even is. A person working with a large language model, a retrieval system, or a notebook of prompts is, on this view, a coupled cognitive system whose boundaries cross the human and the machine, which makes the relevant unit of intelligence the human-plus-tool rather than either alone. It dissolves the sharp line between using an AI and thinking with one, and suggests that the cognitive enhancements of the coming decades may be best understood not as smarter humans or smarter machines in isolation but as extended minds, hybrid systems whose competence belongs to the whole loop. It also sharpens questions of credit, responsibility, and identity: if the tool is part of the mind, the achievements and the failures are partly the tool's, and the self that thinks is already, in Clark's phrase, a natural-born cyborg.
